title: Varroa mite count
subtitle: Measuring the mite load before deciding on a treatment threshold.
excerpt: Treating for Varroa without first measuring the infestation is guesswork. A seven-day natural mite drop count or a sugar roll gives a reliable figure to work from. This covers both methods, how to interpret the results, and when a count indicates treatment is needed.
sourceNotes: NBU Beekeeper's Toolkit (2024), Varroa management and counting. BBKA varroa assessment technical note. SAC Consulting Scotland integrated pest management guidance.

content.text: Every managed honey bee colony in the UK carries

mites. The question is not whether the colony has mites but whether the current population warrants treatment. Treating too early in the season is wasteful and contributes to resistance; treating too late lets mite numbers build to a level that weakens the colony before the treatment takes effect. A count gives you a number to act on rather than a guess.

board into position under the open-mesh floor. Leave it in place for exactly seven days, then remove and count the mites. Mites are pear-shaped, reddish-brown dots, about 1.5 mm across. Divide the total by 7 to get the daily mite drop. A daily drop of 5 to 6 mites in summer is the standard UK treatment threshold; a drop above 10 per day in July or August indicates a heavy infestation that needs treating before the colony builds its winter bees.

gives a faster result and does not depend on counting fallen mites on a board. Fill a 300 ml jar with mesh lid two-thirds full of bees taken from a brood frame (around 300 bees). Add two heaped tablespoons of icing sugar. Roll the jar for two minutes until every bee is coated. Invert the jar over a white surface and shake for 60 seconds: mites fall through the mesh with the sugar. Count the mites. Divide by 3 to get the approximate mite load per 100 bees. Above 3 per 100 bees is the action threshold.

Count in June, before the main summer honey flow. Count again in late July, when you decide whether to treat before the winter bees are raised. A colony treated in August with a licenced product (oxalic acid, Api-Bioxal, Apiguard, Apivar) goes into winter with a much lower mite load than one left alone. Winter losses from Varroa-linked viral disease drop with it.

attrs.body: After the oxalic acid trickle treatment in winter, the colony is broodless and all mites are phoretic (on adult bees). The natural mite drop rate is low, often 0 to 2 per day. This is normal. The treatment is most effective when the colony is broodless; do not interpret a low post-treatment mite drop as a sign the mites have gone.
attrs.title: Counting mites in broodless colonies
